citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. DEXA Data in Football Analytics: The Gridiron Blueprint.
abstract: This framework transforms DEXA scanning from a simple body composition snapshot into a longitudinal predictive model for collegiate football. By engineering novel metrics from serial scans across 100+ football athletes over multiple seasons, it establishes positional body composition fingerprints, quantifies the metabolic cost of a football season through lean mass changes, and introduces composite scores for developmental potential and injury resilience. Key innovations include the Developmental Headroom metric, Core-to-Limb Armor Ratio, and In-Season Catabolic Cost tracking.
tldr: DEXA data becomes a predictive engine when you track rate of change over time instead of just snapshots. First-year lean mass trajectory predicts starter status better than any baseline measurement. The In-Season Catabolic Cost and Core-to-Limb Armor Ratio are new KPIs your nutrition and S&C staff should be tracking.
methods: Longitudinal DEXA analysis across multiple seasons; positional cohort modeling; novel metric engineering (Developmental Headroom, Catabolic Cost, Core-to-Limb Armor Ratio, Developmental Potential Score); statistical modeling of body composition trajectories vs. performance outcomes.
findings: Rate of lean mass change in the first year is a stronger predictor of eventual starter status than any baseline body composition measurement. Bone mineral density declines serve as early markers of systemic overtraining. Visceral adipose tissue spikes during injury rehabilitation create an inflammatory environment that slows healing. Position-specific body composition fingerprints provide actionable benchmarks for recruiting evaluation.
limitations: Internal research document based on a single program's data; positional fingerprints may not generalize across offensive/defensive schemes; DEXA availability and cost limit widespread adoption; longitudinal tracking requires multi-year commitment before predictive models stabilize.
practicalImplications: Track each athlete's rate of lean mass change during their first year as a primary developmental KPI. Calculate In-Season Catabolic Cost (lean mass lost during the competitive season) as a performance indicator for nutrition and S&C staff. Monitor VAT during injury rehab periods. Use the Core-to-Limb Armor Ratio rather than total body mass when evaluating lineman readiness.
athleteDev: Use Developmental Potential Score during recruiting evaluations to project a player's physical ceiling. Stop telling linemen to just 'get bigger' and start targeting core and hip density. Build positional body composition fingerprints as benchmarks for incoming players.
rtp: Monitor VAT spikes during injury rehab as they create inflammatory conditions that slow healing. Track bone mineral density trends as early warning signals for systemic overtraining. Use bilateral DEXA asymmetry flags to identify compensation patterns before they become secondary injuries.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. An Evidence-Based Physical Evaluation Framework for Incoming Collegiate American Football Athletes.
abstract: A comprehensive seven-pillar physical evaluation framework for incoming football athletes (recruits and transfers) using force plates, markerless motion capture, DEXA, and groin strength testing. It synthesizes the evidence on CMJ, IMTP, drop jumps, hip adductor/abductor ratios, and movement screening to replace outdated testing batteries with validated, technology-driven assessments covering Maximal Strength, Explosive Power, Reactive Strength, Movement Efficiency, Body Composition, Musculoskeletal Integrity, and Static Movement Quality.
tldr: Replace your outdated combine-style testing with a seven-pillar evaluation using force plates, markerless motion capture, and DEXA. The groin adductor strength threshold of <465 N is a validated red flag for injury risk. Single-leg CMJ asymmetry and hip adductor/abductor ratios provide more actionable data than any pro agility time ever will.
methods: Literature synthesis of force plate (CMJ, IMTP, drop jump), markerless motion capture (sprint and COD kinematics), DEXA body composition, and groin strength testing protocols; framework development integrating seven assessment domains.
findings: CMJ and IMTP provide reliable, valid measures of explosive power and maximal strength with strong test-retest reliability. Groin adductor strength below 465 N is a validated threshold for elevated injury risk. Single-leg CMJ asymmetry indexes detect bilateral deficits that traditional testing misses. Markerless motion capture during sprints and COD provides movement quality data without lab constraints.
limitations: Framework requires significant technology investment (force plates, markerless motion capture, DEXA); normative data is program-specific and requires calibration period; transfer portal athletes may have limited baseline data; single assessment point captures state not trait.
practicalImplications: Build incoming player evaluation around seven pillars: Maximal Strength (IMTP), Explosive Power (CMJ), Reactive Strength (Drop Jump), Movement Efficiency (markerless motion capture), Body Composition (DEXA), Musculoskeletal Integrity (groin strength + single-leg CMJ asymmetry), and Static Movement Quality (overhead squat via motion capture). Prioritize groin strength screening for all incoming players.
athleteDev: Use the seven-pillar profile to create individualized development plans for each incoming athlete. Identify the weakest pillar and prioritize it in offseason programming. Track pillar scores longitudinally to quantify physical development across a career.
rtp: Use the incoming evaluation as the pre-injury baseline for all RTP decisions. Groin adductor strength <465 N at any point should trigger a modified training plan. Single-leg CMJ asymmetry >15% post-injury should delay full clearance regardless of other tests.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. SpeedSig: From Practitioner's Problem to Validated Product (Review of Weber et al.).
abstract: A research deep dive into SpeedSig, an Australian SaaS analytics platform that repurposes existing Catapult or STATSports GPS hardware (repositioned from thoracic to lumbar spine via custom belt) to generate biomechanical 'Speed Signatures.' The review covers peer-reviewed validation studies showing 'Good' validity (ICC 0.83) for ground contact time against force plates and 'Excellent' field reliability (ICC 0.91-0.92).
tldr: SpeedSig turns your existing GPS hardware into a biomechanics tool by repositioning it to the lumbar spine. Its ground contact time metric is validated against force plates (ICC 0.83) with excellent field reliability (ICC 0.91-0.92). No new hardware purchase required. The real value is in rehab and RTP: objective, on-field biomechanical data showing whether an athlete is truly moving like their healthy self.
methods: Review of peer-reviewed validation studies; ICC analysis for concurrent validity against force plates; test-retest reliability assessment in field conditions; biomechanical signal processing from lumbar-mounted IMU data.
findings: Ground contact time validity rated 'Good' (ICC 0.83) against force plates. Field reliability rated 'Excellent' (ICC 0.91-0.92). Lumbar placement enables 3D profiling of spine, pelvis, and legs independently during running. Software generates individualized 'Speed Signatures' that can be compared pre/post injury for objective RTP assessment.
limitations: Validation studies primarily on straight-line running; limited peer-reviewed data on multidirectional movements; requires custom lumbar belt (proprietary); algorithm details are proprietary; still emerging technology with a small but growing evidence base.
practicalImplications: Move one GPS unit from the thoracic mount to a custom lumbar belt during warm-ups to generate biomechanical data. Use ground contact time, stiffness, and efficiency metrics to track movement quality during rehab progressions. Compare post-injury Speed Signatures to healthy baselines for objective RTP clearance.
athleteDev: Establish healthy Speed Signatures for each athlete during preseason as a baseline for longitudinal monitoring. Use asymmetry and efficiency metrics to identify movement pattern changes that may indicate fatigue accumulation or developing compensation strategies.
rtp: This is SpeedSig's primary value. When an athlete passes strength tests but something still 'looks off,' SpeedSig quantifies what the coaching staff is seeing. Compare post-injury Speed Signatures to the athlete's healthy baseline to verify they are truly moving the way they need to on the field.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. The NIRS-Enhanced Return-to-Play Framework: MOXY Monitor for Football RTP.
abstract: A four-phase RTP framework using MOXY near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) sensors to objectively track local muscle oxygen saturation (SmO2) and total hemoglobin (THb) during rehabilitation. Provides a non-invasive, real-time window into muscle metabolism. Phase 1 covers early rehab bilateral monitoring, Phase 2 adds loaded movement patterns, Phase 3 introduces sport-specific conditioning, and Phase 4 validates full-contact readiness against pre-injury baselines.
tldr: Put a MOXY sensor on the same muscle of both legs during rehab. The uninjured leg is your real-time control. If the injured quad only desaturates 5% during a leg extension while the healthy side drops 20%, the athlete is offloading even if they say it 'feels fine.' SmO2 reoxygenation time between efforts is your primary metric for between-play recovery capacity.
methods: Four-phase protocol design using bilateral MOXY NIRS monitoring; SmO2 and THb signal analysis during therapeutic exercises; reoxygenation rate calculations; pre-injury vs. post-injury NIRS signature comparison framework.
findings: Bilateral NIRS monitoring during early rehab reveals offloading patterns invisible to visual observation. SmO2 desaturation magnitude reflects true muscular work capacity, not just force production. Reoxygenation time between efforts directly correlates with between-play recovery capacity. Sport-specific drill NIRS signatures can be compared to pre-injury baselines.
limitations: MOXY sensors are relatively new to team sport settings; limited normative data for football-specific exercises; sensor placement and skin pigmentation can affect signal quality; requires pre-injury baseline data; cost per unit may limit bilateral monitoring across a full roster.
practicalImplications: Implement bilateral MOXY monitoring from Phase 1 of rehab. Use SmO2 desaturation magnitude (not just force output) to assess true muscular engagement. Track reoxygenation time as the primary metric for between-play recovery capacity. Compare sport-specific drill NIRS signatures to pre-injury baselines before clearing for full contact.
athleteDev: Establish healthy NIRS profiles during preseason testing. Use SmO2 response patterns during conditioning to identify athletes with poor local muscular endurance who may benefit from targeted aerobic development work at the tissue level.
rtp: The core use case. Bilateral NIRS monitoring catches compensation and offloading patterns that strength tests miss. An athlete who produces adequate force but does so through altered metabolic strategies is not truly ready. Do not clear based on strength alone.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. S2 Cognition in College Football.
abstract: Analysis of S2 Cognition's nine-domain cognitive assessment platform for collegiate football applications. S2 measures perception speed, tracking capacity, decision complexity, impulse control, and five other game-speed cognitive skills. Peer-reviewed research shows football players demonstrate measurable cognitive advantages over non-athletes. For QBs, S2 overall score explains 28.7% of career passer rating variance (vs. Wonderlic at 0.01%). For WRs, Search Efficiency and Impulse Control account for 72.2% of drop rate variance.
tldr: S2 Cognition quantifies the 'intangibles' coaches talk about. The QB overall score explains 28.7% of passer rating variance, making the Wonderlic's 0.01% look irrelevant. For receivers, Search Efficiency and Impulse Control predict 72.2% of drop rate variance. Use it as a tie-breaker in recruiting and as a diagnostic tool for player development.
methods: Review of S2 Cognition validation studies; regression analysis of cognitive scores vs. on-field performance metrics (passer rating, drop rate); position-specific cognitive profiling; comparison to Wonderlic.
findings: S2 overall score explains 28.7% of career passer rating variance for QBs. Wonderlic explains only 0.01%. WR Search Efficiency and Impulse Control predict 72.2% of drop rate variance. Collegiate football players show cognitive advantages over non-athletes. Position-specific cognitive profiles emerge with meaningful differences.
limitations: S2 is a proprietary commercial product; independent replication is limited; sample sizes in published validation studies are moderate; cognitive profiles may interact with scheme complexity; cost per assessment limits roster-wide deployment.
practicalImplications: Use S2 as a tie-breaker in recruiting, not standalone. When a receiver keeps dropping balls in traffic, check his Search Efficiency score. If low, design drills with deliberate visual clutter rather than saying 'focus harder.' Use cognitive profiles for scheme-fit analysis.
athleteDev: Design position-specific cognitive development drills based on S2 profiles. If QBs score low on Decision Complexity, simplify read progressions or lean into RPO concepts. Build 'chaos catching' drills for WRs with low Search Efficiency scores.
rtp: Post-concussion S2 reassessment provides an objective cognitive baseline comparison beyond standard ImPACT testing. Domain-specific declines from pre-injury baseline can inform targeted cognitive rehabilitation activities.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. Wearable Resistance Training Program Review: Exogen for Football.
abstract: Review of wearable resistance (WR) technology, specifically Exogen, for speed and COD training in elite football. WR applied to the limbs at 1-5% body mass provides highly specific overload for sprint and COD work. Distal (shank) placement creates greater rotational inertia than proximal (thigh), specifically overloading the deceleration/braking phase of cutting. 8-week warm-up integration improved 10m and 20m sprint times by ~2% without impairing subsequent training quality.
tldr: Exogen calf sleeves integrated into warm-ups 2-3x/week at 1-5% body mass improve sprint times by about 2% over 8 weeks without hurting session quality. Start proximal (near knee) at 200g for weeks 1-3, then go distal (near ankle) at 400-600g in weeks 5-8. Use contrast sets: one loaded rep, then one unloaded at max intent. This is your in-season speed 'microdosing' strategy.
methods: Literature review of wearable resistance research; RCT data from 8-week warm-up integration protocols; biomechanical analysis of proximal vs. distal load placement; effect size calculations for sprint and COD improvements.
findings: 8-week warm-up integration improved 10m and 20m sprint times by ~2%. Distal placement specifically overloads the deceleration/braking phase. WR at 1-5% body mass does not impair subsequent training quality. Contrast sets produce acute post-activation potentiation. 180-degree COD showed greatest responsiveness.
limitations: Most WR research conducted in soccer and rugby, not American football specifically; long-term (>8 week) retention unclear; optimal loading may vary by position and body mass; limited injury risk data; athlete compliance with placement matters.
practicalImplications: Integrate Exogen calf sleeves into RAMP warm-ups 2-3 days/week. Progressive loading: proximal at 200g (weeks 1-3), distal at 400-600g (weeks 5-8). Use contrast sets for acute potentiation. In-season compatible strategy that builds speed without gym fatigue.
athleteDev: Use WR as a 'speed microdosing' tool during in-season when gym-based power training volume is reduced. Prioritize 180-degree COD work for DBs and WRs. Build position-specific WR warm-up templates.
rtp: Introduce WR progressively during late-stage RTP as a movement quality challenge. If an athlete compensates under added rotational inertia, they are not ready for full-speed demands. Use as a stress test for movement quality in the final RTP phase.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. L5-S1 Rehab Protocol: Criterion-Based RTP for Post-Microdiscectomy DL.
abstract: A detailed, two-phase rehab protocol for an elite DL with persistent S1 myotomal weakness after lumbar microdiscectomy. Phase I uses NMES + BFR training for neural drive and atrophy with spine-sparing loads. Phase II integrates unilateral resistance training (trap bar DL, single-leg RDLs), criterion-based plyometric progression, and position-specific 'get-off' retraining. RTP clearance requires a multi-domain scorecard: isokinetic strength >90% LSI, functional hops, 3D motion analysis, and I-PRRS >60.
tldr: The biggest risk after disc surgery isn't re-herniation. It's secondary injuries from compensation caused by S1 weakness. A pain-free athlete is not a ready athlete. Use NMES + voluntary contractions to restore neural drive first, then BFR at 20-30% 1RM for hypertrophy without spinal load. Use trap bar DL for all post-op athletes. Never clear on time alone. Use the RTP scorecard.
methods: Case-study protocol design; two-phase progressive rehab; NMES + BFR integration; criterion-based milestones; multi-domain RTP scorecard (isokinetic LSI, functional hops, 3D motion analysis, I-PRRS).
findings: NMES + voluntary contraction restores neural drive faster than voluntary training alone. BFR at 20-30% 1RM produces hypertrophic adaptations without spinal compressive loads. Trap bar DL reduces spinal flexion vs. conventional. Multi-domain scorecard (>90% LSI, hop symmetry, 3D clearance, I-PRRS >60) provides comprehensive readiness assessment.
limitations: Single case study limits generalizability; optimal NMES parameters for S1 myotome not well established; BFR cuff placement near surgical site needs careful consideration; I-PRRS has limited football-specific validation.
practicalImplications: Pair NMES with voluntary contractions in Phase I for all neural drive deficits post-spine surgery. BFR at 20-30% 1RM during early strength phases. Trap bar DL replaces conventional for all post-disc athletes. Multi-domain RTP scorecard is mandatory. Never use time-based clearance.
athleteDev: Development plans after spine surgery must account for neural drive deficit as a rate limiter. Progressive overload timelines will be longer than for musculotendinous injuries. Communicate adjusted expectations clearly to coaching staff.
rtp: Full scorecard: isokinetic >90% LSI, functional hop symmetry, 3D motion analysis showing no compensatory patterns, I-PRRS >60. The biggest clinical mistake is clearing a pain-free athlete who still has measurable S1 weakness. Secondary injuries from compensation are the real threat.

citation: Baylor Applied Sport Science. The Gridiron Blueprint: Actionable Summary for Coaches and Sports Dietitians.
abstract: A coach and dietitian-friendly summary of the full Gridiron Blueprint research. Distills key analytics into three actionable tools: Data-Driven Positional Fingerprints, the Developmental Potential Score (DPS), and the Injury Resilience Dashboard. Rate of change beats baseline measurements for predicting success. In-Season Catabolic Cost is a staff KPI. Declining bone density is an early warning for systemic overtraining.
tldr: Hand this document to your coaches and dietitians. It translates complex body composition analytics into three tools they can use immediately. Biggest coaching takeaway: stop telling linemen to 'get bigger' and start targeting core and hip density. Biggest nutrition takeaway: track VAT during injury rehab because visceral fat spikes create inflammation that slows healing.
methods: Knowledge translation document synthesizing longitudinal DEXA research into practitioner-friendly frameworks; three applied tools (Positional Fingerprints, Developmental Potential Score, Injury Resilience Dashboard).
findings: Rate of change outperforms static baselines for predicting development. In-Season Catabolic Cost varies significantly by position and nutritional support quality. Core-to-Limb Armor Ratio is more meaningful than total mass for lineman readiness. Declining bone mineral density precedes other markers of systemic overtraining.
limitations: Based on single program's research; tools require DEXA access and longitudinal data; positional fingerprints need program-specific calibration; DPS has not been independently validated.
practicalImplications: Distribute to coaching and nutrition staff as an executive summary. Use three tools immediately. Make In-Season Catabolic Cost a nutrition staff KPI. Stop using total body mass as a lineman readiness metric.
athleteDev: Use DPS during recruiting to project physical ceiling. Build positional fingerprints as benchmarks. Track first-year rate of lean mass change as the primary developmental metric.
rtp: The Injury Resilience Dashboard integrates body composition signals (asymmetry, bone density, VAT) into a single view for medical and performance staff. VAT monitoring during rehab should be standard protocol.

citation: Dixon B, Alexander J, Harper D. 'Post-rehabilitation phase' in professional football: are we optimising player support after return to play? Br J Sports Med. 2025;0:1-3.
abstract: This editorial proposes a formalized 'post-rehabilitation phase' that runs concurrently with grass phases 4–6 of football rehabilitation frameworks following return to training. The authors argue that rehabilitation does not end at RTP, and that the heightened subsequent injury risk in the first month post-RTP (approximately 2x baseline, decaying over ~25 days) demands a structured monitoring and programming phase. The proposed phase operates along two tracks: re-injury mitigation (site-specific workload tolerance, strength/power maintenance, ROM, movement quality) and subsequent injury mitigation (training programmes targeting anatomically linked high-risk sites, GPS workload monitoring, neuromuscular screening).
tldr: Medical clearance is not the end of rehabilitation — a formalized post-rehabilitation phase running alongside grass phases 4–6 is needed to address the significantly elevated subsequent injury risk that persists for approximately one month after return to play in professional football.
methods: Editorial/opinion piece synthesizing existing RTP framework literature and subsequent injury risk data to propose a novel post-rehabilitation phase model. Includes a schematic framework (Figure 1) with re-injury mitigation and subsequent injury mitigation pathways.
findings:
• Subsequent injury risk is approximately 2x baseline immediately post-RTP, decaying over ~25 days with a '1-month risk decay'
• Reinjury rates for specific injuries in professional football are high: 12–43% for hamstring, 31–50% for groin, 30–40% for knee sprains
• Current RTP frameworks treat clearance as a decision point rather than a continuum
• The proposed post-rehabilitation phase includes site-specific workload monitoring, daily pre-training preparation, neuromuscular diagnostics, nutritional monitoring, and psychological readiness tracking
• GPS monitoring, weekly strength/power screening, and gait/neuromuscular analysis are recommended monitoring tools for both re-injury and subsequent injury pathways
limitations: Editorial without primary data; proposed phase is theoretical and requires empirical validation; research is needed on the association between post-RTP load and subsequent injury risk; contextual factors (phase of season, player status, positional switching, coaching team influence) not yet incorporated into the model.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and sport scientists should not discharge athletes from structured monitoring at the point of RTP medical clearance. Implement site-specific daily pre-training preparation, GPS workload monitoring, and weekly strength/power screening for all athletes in the first month post-RTP. Use GPS data and neuromuscular screening to identify athletes at elevated subsequent injury risk due to anatomically linked vulnerability patterns.
athleteDev: For American football and professional soccer programs, this editorial highlights the need for a systematic post-RTP monitoring phase during the first 4–6 weeks after return. NFL and collegiate programs should build post-RTP protocols that address not only reinjury at the index site but also increased vulnerability at anatomically linked sites (e.g., ACL injury increasing hamstring risk). Continuity of individual monitoring from rehab into the post-rehabilitation phase is essential.
rtp: Core contribution: the post-rehabilitation phase concept directly extends RTP practice beyond medical clearance. The re-injury mitigation track monitors workload tolerance, maintains index-site strength and movement quality, and tracks psychological readiness. The subsequent injury mitigation track profiles anatomically linked high-risk sites and prescribes preventive programming. Both tracks run concurrently with grass phases 4–6 until the player achieves pre-injury performance level or higher.

citation: Rodeo SA, Bedi A. 2019-2020 NFL and NFL Physician Society Orthobiologics Consensus Statement. Sports Health. 2020;12(1):58-60.
abstract: This consensus statement from the NFL and NFL Physician Society addresses the use of orthobiologic treatments — including platelet-rich plasma (PRP) and stem cell therapies — in professional football. PRP is produced by centrifuging a patient's blood to concentrate platelets and growth factors that may improve healing. While basic science supports potential for tissue regeneration, current clinical evidence is limited, and only specific PRP formulations have demonstrated efficacy for knee osteoarthritis and patellar/elbow tendinitis. Stem cell therapies available in the United States are unproven for musculoskeletal tissue regeneration, and indiscriminate use may put athlete health at risk.
tldr: NFL team physicians recognize the promise of PRP and stem cell therapies for musculoskeletal injuries but emphasize that clinical evidence remains limited and indiscriminate use without rigorous protocols may harm athletes.
methods: Consensus statement based on review of existing clinical literature and basic science on PRP and stem cell therapies, authored by NFL-affiliated orthopaedic surgeons.
findings:
• PRP has rigorous support only for knee osteoarthritis symptoms and chronic patellar/elbow extensor tendinitis
• PRP has not been reliably shown to regenerate cartilage or improve tendon/meniscal healing
• Stem cell preparations available in the US contain very few true stem cells (approx. 1 in 10,000 bone marrow cells)
• No high-quality evidence supports routine stem cell use for any musculoskeletal injury
• Indiscriminate or non-standardized use of orthobiologics can paradoxically put athlete health at risk
• NFL physicians are committed to studying and reporting use of these treatments responsibly
limitations: Consensus statement rather than primary research; relies on existing literature quality; rapid evolution of the field may outpace recommendations; commercial interests and direct-to-consumer marketing create information gaps.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and sport scientists should critically evaluate claims about PRP and stem cell therapies; only use orthobiologic treatments with clear clinical indications; standardize protocols for preparation and application; track outcomes systematically; do not rely on marketing claims.
athleteDev: Relevant to recovery from soft tissue injuries common in American football (muscle strains, tendon injuries, ligament sprains, cartilage damage). NFL team physicians are actively studying these agents for difficult-to-treat conditions like degenerative disc disease, failed healing fractures, and ligament/meniscal repair in the NFL context.
rtp: Orthobiologic treatments may accelerate return to play for NFL athletes with tendinopathies, cartilage injuries, and soft tissue damage, but only when applied with appropriate indications and rigorous protocols. Premature or poorly-standardized use risks athlete safety and unpredictable outcomes.

citation: Nguyen Q, Yurko R. A Bayesian circular mixed-effects model for explaining variability in directional movement in American football. arXiv:2507.06122. 2025.
abstract: This paper introduces a Bayesian circular mixed-effects model to quantify in-game change-of-direction ability for NFL ball carriers using player tracking data from the NFL Big Data Bowl 2025 (first nine weeks of the 2022 regular season). The authors define 'turn angle' as the angular deviation between consecutive displacement vectors at 10 Hz tracking resolution, and model it using a von Mises distribution with both mean and concentration parameters. Player-level random effects nested within position groups allow estimation of individual variability in directional movement. The framework identifies which running backs, wide receivers, and tight ends exhibit the most erratic (shifty) versus consistent directional behavior on the field.
tldr: Using NFL player tracking data, this paper provides the first public statistical framework to objectively rate ball carrier change-of-direction ability in live game conditions, revealing meaningful positional and individual differences in turning behavior.
methods: Bayesian circular mixed-effects model using von Mises distribution for frame-level turn angle (angle between consecutive displacement vectors at 10 Hz). Data: 9,480 plays across 136 games (weeks 1-9, 2022 NFL season) including 5,431 rushing and 4,049 passing plays. Fixed effects include ball carrier speed, acceleration, cumulative distance, play type, and position; random intercepts for ball carrier nested by position. Implemented in Stan via brms package in R using MCMC with 4 chains, 3,500 iterations, 1,500 burn-in.
findings:
• Higher ball carrier speed is associated with more consistent directional movement (less turning variability)
• Higher acceleration is associated with more variable turning behavior — consistent with cut moves
• Run plays show more concentrated (less variable) turn angles than pass plays
• Wide receivers show more directional variability than running backs; tight ends are intermediate
• Justice Hill (RB) showed the most turn angle variability among running backs; Leonard Fournette and Jonathan Taylor showed the least
• George Pickens showed the most variability among wide receivers; DK Metcalf the least (favors straight-line speed)
• George Kittle, Kyle Pitts, and Travis Kelce ranked among most variable tight ends
• Turning variability and 40-yard dash speed show only a weak positive correlation (r = 0.135)
limitations: Analysis limited to nine weeks of one NFL season; model applied only to ball carriers (RBs after handoff, WRs/TEs after catch); positional coverage excludes QBs, defensive players, and special teams returners; proprietary tracking methods may differ from public approach; uncertainty in estimates for players with few plays.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists and performance staff can use turn angle variability as an objective, in-game metric to profile player change-of-direction ability beyond static Combine drills (3-cone, shuttle). Identifying players with high versus low turning variability informs position-specific training priorities and talent evaluation in scouting contexts.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL and collegiate American football player evaluation and development. The turn angle variability metric quantifies agility and shiftiness in live game conditions for RBs, WRs, and TEs — complementing traditional speed and agility testing. Can guide individualized training to develop or leverage each player's movement profile.
rtp: Potential application in RTP monitoring: tracking a recovering ball carrier's turn angle variability during return to practice or simulated game scenarios could indicate whether their in-game movement patterns have returned to pre-injury baseline, serving as a functional movement readiness marker.

citation: Dairi I, Brown C, DiGregorio H, et al. A case report of reversible cognitive decline in a former professional American-style football player: Findings from the Football Players Health Study In-Person Assessments. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2023;22(5):154-157.
abstract: This case report from the Football Players Health Study (FPHS) at Harvard presents a 54-year-old former NFL linebacker with a high concussion exposure history who was initially diagnosed with frontotemporal dementia (FTD) based on cognitive impairment, behavioral changes, and personality changes. Comprehensive multi-modal assessment including MRI revealed mild hydrocephalus — enlargement of the lateral and third ventricles — rather than frontotemporal atrophy. Following ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement in February 2020, the patient experienced marked improvement in mood and resolution of most cognitive complaints, demonstrating that the initial FTD diagnosis was incorrect. The case highlights the importance of thorough differential diagnosis in former football players presenting with cognitive decline.
tldr: A former NFL player initially diagnosed with frontotemporal dementia was found to have treatable normal pressure hydrocephalus confirmed by MRI, with dramatic cognitive improvement following ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement — underscoring the need for comprehensive differential diagnosis in athletes with cognitive complaints.
methods: Single case report from the FPHS In-Person Assessment protocol: 3-day multi-modality assessment including cardiovascular, neurocognitive, endocrinologic, sleep, and chronic pain evaluations; T-MoCA telephone screening; brain MRI; EEG; sleep study; laboratory and cardiovascular testing; structured exit interview with physician and nurse.
findings:
• 54-year-old former NFL linebacker with concussion symptom score of 82 (high exposure) and T-MoCA score of 17 (mild cognitive impairment)
• Initial diagnosis of early onset FTD based on cognitive and behavioral symptoms
• Brain MRI showed mild enlargement of lateral and third ventricles with balloon-like rounding of frontal horns — consistent with hydrocephalus, not frontotemporal atrophy
• EEG showed nonspecific cortical slowing
• Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed February 2020 resulted in marked improvement in mood and resolution of most cognitive complaints
• Normal pressure hydrocephalus should be considered in differential diagnosis for former football players with cognitive decline
• Multiple comorbidities (sleep apnea, low HDL, low vitamin D, mildly impaired exercise capacity) were identified
limitations: Single case report; cannot establish causality or prevalence; the role of football participation in development of hydrocephalus is unknown; prevalence of hydrocephalus specifically in former ASF players has not been reported; diagnosis of NPH in athletes may be confused with CTE given symptom overlap.
practicalImplications: Medical staff and clinicians evaluating former football players with cognitive decline, behavioral changes, or personality changes should include normal pressure hydrocephalus in the differential diagnosis. Brain MRI should be obtained when evaluating new-onset dementia symptoms. Treatable conditions must be ruled out before attributing decline to CTE or FTD.
athleteDev: Relevant to long-term health monitoring of American football players at NFL and collegiate levels. High cumulative concussion exposure in football players may mask or co-occur with other treatable neurological conditions. Comprehensive post-career health screening programs (like the FPHS model) can identify actionable diagnoses.
rtp: Not directly applicable to acute RTP decisions. However, this case illustrates the importance of thorough neurological evaluation in athletes with cognitive symptoms and high concussion histories before concluding that symptoms are permanent — relevant to concussion protocol management and post-career athlete health programs.

citation: Baker HP, Tjong VK, Varelas A, Wonais M, Terry MA. A case series of pectoralis major injuries on one collegiate football team. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2017;16(5):346-350.
abstract: This case series examines seven pectoralis major muscle (PMM) ruptures occurring on a single collegiate varsity football team over a 5-year period (2011–2015). All injuries occurred during bench press in the weight room — an incidence of 0.025 per team per year, nearly 10 times the rate reported for NFL athletes. The authors hypothesize that concurrent upper-body strength and endurance training, combined with athletes attempting personal record bench press lifts with pre-existing lower-extremity injuries restricting them to upper-body-only training, contributed to this unusual clustering. Grade II ruptures were most common and managed nonoperatively; one grade III rupture required surgery and led to medical retirement.
tldr: Seven pectoralis major ruptures occurred on one collegiate football team over five years, all during bench press, at nearly 10 times the NFL incidence rate — highlighting the risk of upper-body overload in college athletes restricted from lower-body training and the dangers of frequent maximal bench press attempts.
methods: Retrospective case series review of all PMM injuries in varsity football team members at one collegiate institution (2011–2015). Chart review by medical and training room staff; MRI confirmation of diagnosis; injury graded using Tietjen classification (Grade I–III). Treatment, return-to-sport days, practices/games missed, and period of injury recorded.
findings:
• 7 PMM ruptures over 5 years on one team; incidence of 0.025/team/year (~10x the NFL rate of 0.004/year)
• All injuries occurred during bench press in the weight room (off-season or preseason)
• Positions: 4 defensive linemen, 2 offensive linemen, 1 defensive back
• Grade distribution: 4 Grade II (partial rupture), 1 Grade III (complete rupture requiring surgery — medically retired), 2 Grade I/II
• Return to sport ranged from 21 to 122 days; most Grade II injuries returned in 42–86 days
• 6 of 7 athletes had pre-existing lower-body injuries at time of PMM injury, restricting them to upper-body training
• All athletes were attempting personal record bench press lifts at time of injury
limitations: Single institution retrospective case series; small sample (n=7); non-randomized design; findings may not generalize beyond collegiate football; confounded by team-specific strength and conditioning protocols.
practicalImplications: Limit maximal (1RM) bench press attempts to 2–3 times per year. Modify upper-body training loads for athletes restricted to upper-body-only work due to lower-extremity injury — avoid concurrent upper-body strength and endurance overload. Monitor volume and intensity of bench press in off-season and preseason blocks.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American collegiate and NFL football S&C programs. Linemen and large skill players are at highest risk for PMM rupture. Off-season and preseason weight room programming should include load management for bench press, especially for athletes with concurrent lower-extremity restrictions.
rtp: Grade I–II PMM ruptures managed nonoperatively typically allow return to sport in 3–12 weeks with sling immobilization, progressive passive-to-active exercise, and full unrestricted activity at 6 weeks. Grade III complete ruptures requiring surgery had significantly longer recovery (122 days in this series). RTP criteria should include full pain-free ROM, symmetrical strength on adduction and internal rotation, and sport-specific functional testing.

citation: Calleja-González J, Mallo J, Cos F, et al. A commentary of factors related to player availability and its influence on performance in elite team sports. Front Sports Act Living. 2023;4:1077934.
abstract: This general commentary reviews the multifactorial determinants of player availability and their influence on performance in elite team sports. Player availability — defined as athletes being injury-free and ready to compete — is argued to be more important than individual peak performance during match-congested schedules. The authors examine how match congestion, injuries, fatigue, travel, sleep disruption, and high-status player absences collectively degrade team performance. Practical recommendations spanning workload monitoring, recovery strategies, squad rotation, travel management, return-to-sport decision-making, and interdisciplinary communication are provided.
tldr: Keeping players available and injury-free matters more than peak individual performance during congested schedules; sport science staff should prioritize personalized workload monitoring, proactive recovery, and robust internal communication to maximize player availability.
methods: Narrative commentary and review of existing literature synthesizing evidence across basketball, soccer, futsal, volleyball, Australian football, and rugby on player availability, injury incidence, match congestion, travel fatigue, sleep, and return-to-sport decision-making.
findings:
• Fewer injuries are consistently associated with higher final league rankings across multiple team sports
• Match congestion increases injury and illness risk, particularly when inter-match recovery is less than 96 hours
• High-status players disproportionately affect team performance; keeping them available is paramount
• Frequent long-haul travel compounds fatigue and sleep disruption, impairing performance and health
• Good internal communication between medical staff and head coaches reduces injury burden
• Larger squad depth and appropriate rotation reduce cumulative load and injury risk during congested periods
• Return-to-sport decisions are complex and often misaligned between players, coaches, and medical staff
limitations: Commentary is opinion-based without systematic search or meta-analytic methodology; evidence quality across cited studies varies; most data originate from soccer and basketball, limiting generalizability to all team sports.
practicalImplications: Implement daily individualized monitoring of fitness, workload, and well-being; prioritize post-game and mid-week recovery; use larger squads for rotation during congested periods; establish clear return-to-sport criteria co-developed by medical and coaching staff; improve interdisciplinary communication protocols.
athleteDev: For American football programs, player availability during a congested schedule is more valuable than chasing individual peak performance. Developing depth at all positions, standardizing workload monitoring, and protecting high-status players from unnecessary exposure should be organizational priorities.
rtp: RTP decision-making is highlighted as a complex, high-stakes process prone to disagreement between players, coaches, and medical staff. The commentary recommends creating individualized prevention and corrective training programs and improving communication among all stakeholders when clearing athletes for return.

citation: Ichesco E, Li Y, Shih C-H, et al. A comparison of brain MRI outcomes in youth American football versus noncontact sport athletes. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2026;58(2):261-270.
abstract: This secondary analysis of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study compared brain MRI outcomes between 9- to 10-year-old American football players (n=1,194) and noncontact sport controls (n=807). Structural MRI, resting-state functional MRI, and diffusion tensor imaging were analyzed. Primary analyses identified only one significant post-hoc finding: smaller cortical thickness in the left anterior transverse collateral sulcus in the football group. No significant differences in hippocampal volumes, network-to-network connectivity, or diffusion metrics were localized to individual regions after FDR correction. The authors conclude that participation in American football at ages 9–10 is associated with minimal MRI differences compared to noncontact sport peers.
tldr: In the largest neuroimaging study of youth football players to date, 9- to 10-year-old American football athletes showed minimal differences in brain structure, functional connectivity, and diffusion MRI compared to noncontact sport athletes, providing some reassurance about early contact sport participation while noting the need for longitudinal follow-up.
methods: Secondary cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Study Release 5.0 baseline neuroimaging data. Multivariate general linear models compared football (n=1,194) vs. noncontact sport controls (n=807) across structural MRI (74 cortical thickness parcels, 10 subcortical volumes), rs-fMRI, and diffusion MRI (21 measures). Covariates included age, sex, race, handedness, household income, study site, and TBI history. FDR correction applied.
findings:
• Only one significant post-hoc finding: smaller left anterior transverse collateral sulcus cortical thickness in football players (Cohen d = −0.258)
• Global effects on cortical thickness, network-to-network connectivity, and fiber tract volume present in primary multivariate analyses but no individual regions survived FDR correction
• No significant differences in subcortical or hippocampal volumes between groups
• Nonsignificant trend toward lower hippocampal volumes with increasing years of football participation
• No significant dose-effect relationships between years of football play and any MRI outcome after FDR correction
limitations: Cross-sectional design; self-reported sport participation data does not distinguish tackle from flag football; TBI identification may undercount concussion history; MRI data collected across 21 sites; study focused only on MRI outcomes without cognitive or behavioral measures.
practicalImplications: These findings offer partial reassurance regarding early youth football participation, suggesting minimal measurable brain MRI differences at ages 9–10. However, the hippocampal volume trend underscores the importance of longitudinal monitoring and continued research. Staff should maintain rigorous concussion protocols and track head impact exposure.
athleteDev: The hippocampal volume trend with more years of play warrants continued monitoring of athletes with extensive exposure histories. Programs should advocate for graduated contact exposure at youth levels and robust concussion education.
rtp: The small but present global MRI effects support continued caution in RTP decision-making following concussion in youth football players. Clinicians should not assume absence of gross MRI findings equates to full neurological recovery.

citation: Fitzgerald CF, Jensen RL. A comparison of the National Football League's annual National Football League Combine 1999–2000 to 2015–2016. J Strength Cond Res. 2020;34(3):771-781.
abstract: This cross-sectional study compared NFL Combine performance data from 1999–2000 (n=635) to 2015–2016 (n=628) across eight position groups to determine whether elite football players have become bigger, faster, and stronger over a 17-year span. Statistically significant improvements were found in the 40-yard dash, vertical jump, broad jump, and 3-cone drill across the full sample, with no significant changes in height or overall body mass. The broad jump was the only test where every position showed a significant difference between combine years, suggesting broad improvements in lower-body explosive power.
tldr: Elite NFL Combine prospects in 2015–2016 were faster, more explosive, and more agile than their 1999–2000 counterparts, with the broad jump improving across all positions, supporting the notion that elite football players continue to evolve physically over time.
methods: Two-way non-repeated ANOVA comparing combine performance (40-yd dash, pro-agility shuttle, 225-lb bench press reps, vertical jump, broad jump, 3-cone drill, height, body mass) between the 1999–2000 and 2015–2016 NFL Combine cohorts, separated by position group. Data sourced from public NFL historical databases.
findings:
• 40-yard dash improved significantly overall (99–00: 4.85s vs. 15–16: 4.80s; p=0.002)
• Vertical jump improved significantly (99–00: 82.04 cm vs. 15–16: 83.46 cm; p=0.028)
• Broad jump improved significantly for all eight positions (p<0.001 overall)
• 3-cone drill improved significantly (99–00: 7.41s vs. 15–16: 7.29s; p<0.001)
• No significant differences in overall height or body mass between combine years
• Position-specific body mass increases found for QBs, RBs, WRs, and TEs
• No significant differences in NFL 225-lb bench press reps overall
limitations: Cross-sectional design; cannot account for changes in combine preparation strategies between eras; athletes chose which tests to complete (selection bias); body composition not directly measured; data from public databases with potential entry errors.
practicalImplications: S&C staff can use these normative combine data to benchmark current athletes against evolving standards. Modern training and nutrition practices are producing measurable gains in speed and lower-body explosive power. Prioritize broad jump and sprint training as these are the metrics most universally improving.
athleteDev: Position-specific normative data enable more precise evaluation of prospects. Consistent improvement in broad jump across all positions highlights lower-body explosive power as a universal performance driver. Athletes can set position-specific combine benchmarks as developmental targets.
rtp: Combine data inform return-to-sport performance benchmarks. Following lower-extremity injury, performance targets in the 40-yard dash, broad jump, and vertical jump should be referenced against position-specific combine norms to assess whether an athlete has returned to an elite performance standard prior to full clearance.

citation: Lalor BJ, Halson SL, Tran J, Kemp JG, Cormack SJ. A complex relationship: sleep, external training load, and well-being in elite Australian footballers. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2019. DOI: 10.1123/ijspp.2019-0061.
abstract: This study examined the relationships between objective sleep characteristics, external training load, and subjective well-being ratings in 38 elite male Australian football (AF) players over a 15-day preseason monitoring period. Sleep was assessed via wrist actigraphy and sleep diaries, external load via GPS/accelerometry (Catapult), and well-being via a daily Likert-scale questionnaire. Canonical correlation analysis revealed moderate-to-strong relationships, with fragmented pretraining sleep associated with higher player load 2D during training and lower subjective well-being.
tldr: Fragmented pretraining sleep in elite Australian footballers is associated with higher-intensity movement patterns during training and lower well-being; practitioners should prioritize sleep quality and minimize early morning training start times, particularly after high-speed sessions, to optimize player readiness.
methods: Prospective observational study of 38 elite male AF players over a 15-day preseason period. Objective sleep via Philips Respironics actigraphy; external load via Catapult Minimax v4 devices during 5 main field sessions; well-being via 10-item Likert scale (Smartabase app). Canonical correlation analysis and Pearson r used.
findings:
• Moderate canonical correlations between pretraining sleep and external training load (r=0.32–0.49)
• Strongest association: well-being and external training load dimension 1 (r=0.67); hamstring soreness was the strongest well-being contributor (r=−0.55)
• Fragmented sleep (more wake bouts, lower sleep efficiency) associated with higher PL2D during training
• Higher maximum speed and PL per minute linked to shorter sleep onset latency and higher sleep efficiency posttraining
• Players with higher PL2D who slept poorly prior to training exhibited lower mood and increased soreness
limitations: Small sample (n=38) from a single team; 15-day monitoring window captures only preseason; actigraphy is not polysomnography; causal direction of relationships cannot be established.
practicalImplications: Monitor player load and PL2D as indicators of posttraining sleep need; schedule training sessions to allow later wake-up times after high-speed sessions; implement sleep hygiene strategies; use morning well-being questionnaires to anticipate load profiles expected during the subsequent session.
athleteDev: Applicable to NFL and collegiate football programs during training camp and in-season periods. Monitoring actigraphy-based sleep alongside GPS load data can help performance staff identify players at risk of accumulated fatigue. Scheduling later practice start times after high-intensity days is an actionable strategy.
rtp: Poor sleep quality was linked to higher injury-risk indicators (increased soreness, lower well-being, less efficient movement). RTP protocols should incorporate sleep monitoring as a readiness indicator; athletes should not be cleared for high-load sessions if pretraining sleep characteristics suggest insufficient recovery.

citation: Galdino M, Wicker P. A culture of constraints: how head coach turnovers affect the backroom staff and player development in professional football. Int J Sports Sci Coach. 2023;1-10.
abstract: This qualitative study, conducted in Brazilian elite football (soccer), examined how within-season head coach turnovers affect player development and create spillover effects for backroom staff. Thirty backroom practitioners completed semi-structured interviews. Reflexive thematic analysis identified an overarching culture of constraints driven by conflicting behaviors and working practices. At the team level: pragmatic playing style, methodological mismatch, and delayed team cohesion. At the individual level: internal disruption, repetitive adaptations, job insecurity, and mental and emotional unrest.
tldr: Within-season head coach turnovers in professional football (soccer) create a culture of constraints that disrupts backroom staff routines, destabilizes player development, increases injury risk through abrupt load changes, and generates significant occupational and emotional stress for the multidisciplinary performance team.
methods: Qualitative study using semi-structured interviews with 30 backroom staff practitioners from Brazilian elite football (Brasileirão) conducted January–March 2021. Interviews averaged 71 minutes; reflexive thematic analysis following Braun and Clarke's framework.
findings:
• Within-season coaching turnovers create a 'culture of constraints' at both team and individual levels
• New coaches adopt pragmatic/defensive playing styles, causing methodological mismatches that disrupt player training adaptation
• Backroom staff experience internal disruption, repetitive adaptations, job insecurity, and mental/emotional unrest
• Abrupt changes in training load and methodology increase risk of physical complaints, fatigue, and muscle injuries
• Internal communication quality degrades with each turnover, compromising injury prevention and player availability protocols
• Incoming coaches sometimes demand injured players return to training before medically appropriate
limitations: Qualitative design limits generalizability; all perspectives from backroom staff rather than head coaches or players; focus exclusively on mid-season replacements; retrospective experiences subject to recall bias.
practicalImplications: Performance and medical staff should advocate for institutional stability and clear internal communication protocols that persist across coaching transitions. Onboarding for new head coaches should include structured orientation to existing medical and performance protocols. Organizations should consider sports psychology support for backroom staff during coaching transitions.
athleteDev: Applicable to American football programs where coordinator or head coach changes are common. Abrupt system changes can disrupt player development timelines, increase soft-tissue injury risk through unaccustomed load patterns, and damage trust between players and performance staff. Continuity of S&C and sports medicine protocols across coaching changes should be a front-office priority.
rtp: The study highlights that coaching turnovers create pressure on backroom staff to return injured players prematurely. Organizations should establish and protect evidence-based RTP criteria that are independent of head coaching turnover decisions.

citation: Swartz EE, Myers JL, Cook SB, et al. A helmetless-tackling intervention in American football for decreasing head impact exposure: a randomized controlled trial. J Sci Med Sport. 2019. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsams.2019.05.018.
abstract: This nested randomized controlled trial evaluated a behavioral intervention (helmetless tackling and blocking drills) to reduce head impact exposure in youth American football players ages 14–17. Participants were assigned to a without-helmet (WoH) tackling/blocking intervention or control group across two 11-week seasons. Head impact frequency per athlete exposure was analyzed using mixed effect models. WoH participants had significantly fewer game-related impacts per athlete exposure at multiple time points across both seasons, with the strongest effects seen in upper-level (10th–11th grade) players.
tldr: Helmetless tackling and blocking drills reduced head impact frequency during games in high school football players, particularly among upper-level athletes, though effects waned by season's end — suggesting dose and experience matter for sustained behavior change.
methods: Nested randomized controlled trial; 180 male high school players (ages 14–17) randomized within teams to WoH intervention or control across 2 seasons. SIM-G head impact sensors worn during all practices and games. Mixed effect models and ANCOVA used to compare impacts per athlete exposure by group, time, and experience level.
findings:
• WoH group had 26–33% fewer game-related impacts per athlete exposure at weeks 4 and 7 across both seasons compared to controls
• Upper-level WoH players had significantly fewer game impacts at weeks 4 and 7 (both seasons)
• Entry-level players showed no significant intervention effect
• Benefits diminished by end-of-season (week 11) for all groups
• Total of 47,382 head impacts analyzed from 115 compliant participants
limitations: Dose-response relationship not fully established; sensor validity limitations for low-cost wearables; perceived risk in helmetless participants not measured; intervention fidelity dependent on coach compliance.
practicalImplications: Incorporating helmetless tackling/blocking drill progressions into practice 2–4x/week may reduce head impact frequency during games, especially for more experienced players. Programs should plan for sustained dose delivery across the full season and account for player experience level when setting expectations.
athleteDev: For collegiate and NFL programs, helmetless tackling drills may reinforce proper head placement and reduce risk-homeostasis behaviors associated with helmet use. Upper-level (more experienced) players respond better, suggesting this intervention is most impactful when introduced early in a player's development and maintained consistently.
rtp: Helmetless drill progressions could be used during RTP to reinforce safe contact mechanics before full-contact clearance, particularly for athletes returning from concussion or cervical injury.

citation: Provencher MT, Bradley JP, Chahla J, et al. A history of anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction at the National Football League Combine results in inferior early National Football League career participation. Arthroscopy. 2018. DOI: 10.1016/j.arthro.2018.03.018.
abstract: This retrospective case-control study evaluated NFL Combine participants (2009–2015) with a prior ACLR (n=110) compared to matched controls (n=300). Players with prior ACLR had significantly worse mean draft pick number and draft round. In the first two NFL seasons, ACLR players started and played significantly fewer games and had lower snap percentages than controls across all positions. Concomitant meniscal or chondral pathology further worsened outcomes.
tldr: NFL players with a prior ACL reconstruction at the Combine played significantly fewer games and had lower snap percentages in their first two NFL seasons compared to uninjured controls, with defensive linemen, defensive backs, and linebackers most affected.
methods: Retrospective case-control study; 2,285 NFL Combine participants (2009–2015) screened; 110 players with isolated ACLR included; matched to 300 controls by position. Outcome metrics: games started, games played, draft pick/round, and snap percentage for seasons 1 and 2. MRI reviewed at Combine for concomitant pathology.
findings:
• ACLR group started significantly fewer games in seasons 1 and 2 (2.7 vs. 5.4 and 5.2 vs. 12.6; both P<.001)
• ACLR group played significantly fewer games in seasons 1 and 2 (9.2 vs. 11.9 and 7.4 vs. 10.4)
• ACLR group had significantly lower snap % in seasons 1 and 2 (21.0% vs. 44.1% and 25.4% vs. 49.4%)
• ACLR group had worse draft round (3.5 vs. 2.7; P=.002) and draft pick number (129.5 vs. 99.3; P=.019)
• Defensive linemen, defensive backs, and linebackers most affected positions
• Combined ACL+meniscal+chondral injury group had lowest participation metrics
limitations: Retrospective design; different physicians evaluated players without standardized laxity measurement; surgical technique, graft type, and rehab protocols not differentiated; time from ACLR to Combine not consistently recorded.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should understand that players with prior ACLR — particularly those with concomitant meniscal or chondral pathology — carry meaningful risk for reduced early-career participation. Draft evaluation, contract structuring, and load management plans should account for position-specific ACLR impact.
athleteDev: ACLR history at the draft/combine stage should prompt individualized development timelines. Players at affected positions (DL, DB, LB) may need extended physical preparation periods and realistic expectations for snap-count progression in years 1–2.
rtp: Prior ACLR significantly reduces early NFL career participation metrics even after passing the Combine medical evaluation. RTP protocols should include snap-count monitoring, functional performance benchmarks by position, and longitudinal tracking of game participation. Concomitant meniscal and chondral pathology should trigger more conservative RTP progressions.

citation: Nguyen Q, Yurko R. A multilevel model with heterogeneous variances for snap timing in the National Football League. arXiv:2502.16313. 2025.
abstract: Using NFL Big Data Bowl 2025 player tracking data from the first nine weeks of the 2022 NFL season, this study developed a Bayesian multilevel model to assess quarterback ability to synchronize ball snap timing with pre-snap receiver motion. Snap timing was defined as the number of tracking frames between the start of a receiver's motion and the ball snap event. A Gaussian mixture model identified six motion-type clusters. Results showed Patrick Mahomes as the top quarterback for snap timing variability, and higher variability in snap timing was associated with less defensive havoc.
tldr: Higher variability in QB snap timing — the time between a receiver going in motion and the ball being snapped — is beneficial for the passing offense, as it makes it harder for defenders to anticipate the snap; Patrick Mahomes ranked first on this metric among NFL quarterbacks.
methods: Bayesian multilevel model with Gamma distribution for snap timing; quarterback random effects modeled on both mean and shape (variance) parameters; Gaussian mixture model (6 clusters) for motion type identification; fitted using brms in R with MCMC sampling; 2,198 passing plays from weeks 1–9 of the 2022 NFL season.
findings:
• Snap timing is right-skewed with median ~17 frames (~1.7 seconds) between motion start and snap
• Later downs and no timeouts remaining associated with longer snap timing
• Wide receivers and tight ends in motion have longer snap timing than running backs
• Higher snap timing variability associated with lower defensive havoc rate
• Patrick Mahomes ranked #1 in snap timing variability among NFL quarterbacks analyzed
• Six motion-type clusters identified with differential effects on mean snap timing
limitations: Tracking data limited to first 9 weeks of 2022 season; motion start identification required custom algorithm; clustering approach may be refined; model does not account for all play-design factors.
practicalImplications: Analytics and coaching staffs can use snap timing variability as a quarterback evaluation metric and play-design tool. Offenses that deliberately vary the cadence between motion and snap create more uncertainty for the defense, potentially reducing disruptive defensive actions.
athleteDev: Developing comfort with variable snap counts in the presence of pre-snap motion is an underutilized QB skill. Training environments that practice snapping at different points in a receiver's motion route may help quarterbacks develop a competitive edge.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play. This is a performance analytics study focused on offensive play design and quarterback evaluation, with no injury or rehabilitation component.

citation: Edwards T, Spiteri T, Piggott B, Haff GG, Joyce C. A narrative review of the physical demands and injury incidence in American football: application of current knowledge and practices in workload management. Sports Med. 2017;48(1):1-18.
abstract: This narrative review synthesized literature on the physical demands and injury incidence of American football and explored how workload management principles from other team sports can be applied. The review details position-specific movement profiles, impact profiles, and work:rest ratios in collegiate American football, finding substantial differences between lineman and non-lineman positions. Injury rates are significantly higher during games than practices. The workload-injury aetiology model is presented as a framework for American football practitioners.
tldr: American football athletes face highly position-specific physical and collision demands, and evidence-based workload management principles — including maintaining moderate chronic loads, limiting weekly spikes to <10%, and monitoring both external and internal load — can be applied to reduce injury risk in this population.
methods: Narrative review; literature synthesized across collegiate and professional American football; position-specific GPS/accelerometer data, impact classification zones, anthropometric data, injury surveillance databases (NCAA 1988–2004), and workload-injury aetiology models from rugby and Australian rules football contextualized for American football.
findings:
• Wide receivers, DBs, and LBs cover the greatest distances and perform most high-speed running efforts; running backs endure the highest severe collision loads (>10 G)
• Game injury rates (~35.9/1000 AEs) are ~9x higher than in-season practice rates (~3.8/1000 AEs) at collegiate level
• Training camp injury rates (13.36/1000 AEs) are >2x collegiate practice rates
• 78% of game injuries and 69% of spring practice injuries caused by player contact
• Most common injuries: meniscal/ACL tears (17.8%), ankle sprains (15.6%), concussion (6.8%)
• Weekly workload increases >10% associated with substantially elevated injury risk
• Chronic workload >4750 AU protective against max-velocity non-contact injury
limitations: Limited American football-specific workload-injury research; most evidence extrapolated from rugby and Australian rules football; only one published study directly linked workload to injury in American football; GPS impact thresholds developed for other sports may not be valid.
practicalImplications: Sport science and athletic training staff should implement athlete monitoring systems using GPS/IMU technology. Weekly workload increases should be kept below 10%; chronic workload should be maintained through off-season and holiday periods; pre-season camp load should be carefully ramped to reduce muscle strain risk. Players not getting game snaps are at elevated injury risk due to low chronic workload.
athleteDev: Position-specific training prescription is essential given large differences in physical characteristics. Non-linemen (WR, DB, LB) should be trained for high-speed running capacity; linemen need collision tolerance and strength. Building high chronic workloads during the off-season prepares athletes to tolerate the demands of training camp and competition.
rtp: The workload-injury aetiology model is directly applicable to American football RTP decisions. Athletes returning from injury should have chronic workload progressively rebuilt before full-contact exposure. Tracking acute:chronic workload ratios and ensuring athletes are not exposed to maximum-velocity efforts before adequate chronic training loads are established are key RTP principles.

citation: Garrett J, Graham SR, Eston RG, et al. A novel method of assessment for monitoring neuromuscular fatigue within Australian rules football players. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2019;14(3):414-420.
abstract: This study compared the sensitivity of a submaximal run test (SRT) using triaxial accelerometer variables to the countermovement jump (CMJ) for detecting neuromuscular fatigue in 23 professional and semi-professional Australian rules football players. Testing was conducted at baseline (24-hours pre-match), 48-hours post-match, and 96-hours post-match. Both CMJ and SRT variables returned to baseline by 96 hours, and the SRT demonstrated comparable or greater sensitivity to the CMJ for detecting neuromuscular fatigue in fatigued athletes.
tldr: A submaximal 50-meter run test using triaxial accelerometer variables detected neuromuscular fatigue in Australian rules footballers as sensitively as the countermovement jump, peaking at 48 hours post-match and resolving by 96 hours — offering a practical, sport-specific monitoring alternative.
methods: 23 professional and semi-professional ARF players tested at baseline (24-hr pre-match), 48-hr post-match, and 96-hr post-match; submaximal run test: 3×50 m runs at standardized 8-second pace; Catapult MinimaxX GPS units measured player load vectors; CMJ height via GymAware optical encoder; participants categorized as 'fatigued' (n=9) or 'non-fatigued' (n=14).
findings:
• Small decrease in CMJ height at 48-hr post-match (ES -0.43, likely) returning to baseline at 96-hr
• Moderate decreases in PL1Dup (ES -0.60, likely) and PL1Dside (ES -0.74, likely) at 48-hr post-match
• 'Fatigued' subgroup (n=9) showed large CMJ reduction (ES -1.42, almost certainly) and moderate SRT vector decreases at 48-hr
• 'Non-fatigued' subgroup showed no meaningful changes in either measure
• SRT demonstrated moderate-to-large sensitivity in fatigued athletes compared to small sensitivity of CMJ overall
• All variables returned to pre-match baseline by 96-hr post-match
limitations: Small sample size (n=23); mix of professional and semi-professional players; recovery strategies not controlled; GPS data quality dependent on satellite lock; study followed normal competition microcycle, limiting generalizability to other scheduling contexts.
practicalImplications: The SRT provides a practical, field-based alternative or complement to CMJ testing for monitoring neuromuscular recovery post-match. Sport science staff can use triaxial accelerometer-derived player load vectors (particularly vertical and medio-lateral) during standardized short runs to identify athletes who have not yet recovered at 48 hours post-match.
athleteDev: This paper focuses on Australian rules football, but the monitoring methodology is highly transferable to NFL and collegiate programs. Implementing a standardized submaximal run test with GPS/accelerometer units could provide a sport-specific neuromuscular fatigue indicator that captures football-relevant movement patterns more than a vertical jump alone.
rtp: The SRT-based monitoring approach offers a practical RTP tool: athletes returning from injury can be assessed for neuromuscular recovery via submaximal running without requiring maximal effort. Tracking the 48-hour and 96-hour post-game accelerometer vectors during a standardized run could help clinicians confirm neuromuscular readiness before progressing RTP protocols.

citation: Willer B, Zivadinov R, Haider MN, Miecznikowski JC, Leddy JJ. A preliminary study of early-onset dementia of former professional football and hockey players. J Head Trauma Rehabil. 2018;33(5):E1-E8.
abstract: This case-control study examined 22 former professional NFL and NHL athletes (average age 56 years) and 21 age-matched noncontact sport athlete controls using comprehensive neuropsychological, executive function, mental health, and advanced brain imaging assessments. No former athlete qualified for a diagnosis of early-onset dementia. Contact sport athletes were more anxious, more likely to report unusual beliefs, and had more physical health problems including sleep disturbance and chronic pain, but objective cognitive and brain imaging measures were largely indistinguishable from controls.
tldr: Despite widespread public concern, comprehensive testing of former NFL and NHL athletes found no evidence of early-onset dementia or significant cognitive differences from noncontact sport athlete controls, suggesting the risk of CTE may not be as universal as media coverage implies.
methods: Case-control design; 22 former NFL/NHL players and 21 noncontact sport controls underwent neuropsychological testing, self-report executive function inventories, psychiatric screening, physical examination, and multimodal MRI (structural, diffusion, susceptibility-weighted, MR spectroscopy, perfusion-weighted) on a 3T scanner. MCI classified using comprehensive criteria.
findings:
• None of the 22 former NFL/NHL athletes met criteria for early-onset dementia
• Contact athletes more likely to qualify as MCI (8/21 vs. 3/21) but difference only trended toward significance (p=0.08)
• No significant differences in objective neuropsychological test scores between groups except Letter Fluency and List B Immediate Recall
• No neurometabolic, hemodynamic, functional, or structural MRI differences between groups
• Contact athletes had significantly more sleep disturbance (18/22), chronic pain (19/22), and higher BMI than controls
• Noncontact controls had more cerebral microbleeds (n=7) than contact athletes (n=2)
limitations: Small convenience sample with potential selection bias toward healthier athletes; noncontact controls were more physically active and better educated; retrospective concussion history excluded; cross-sectional design cannot assess longitudinal decline; represents living athletes, not those who donated brains to brain banks.
practicalImplications: Sport science and medical staff should recognize that public perception of inevitable CTE in contact sport athletes is not supported by objective data in living athlete cohorts. Monitoring for sleep disturbance, chronic pain, obesity, and anxiety may be more clinically urgent for former NFL/NHL players than cognitive screening alone.
athleteDev: For NFL and collegiate American football programs, long-term athlete development policies should not assume inevitable neurocognitive decline, but should address modifiable health factors common in former players: obesity, chronic pain, sleep disorders, and anxiety.
rtp: Not directly applicable to acute RTP decisions, but findings underscore that former NFL athletes' cognitive concerns may relate more to sleep disturbance, chronic pain, and anxiety than to structural brain injury, informing long-term post-career wellness protocols.

citation: Smith NA, Franettovich Smith MM, Bourne MN, Barrett RS, Hides JA. A prospective study of risk factors for hamstring injury in Australian football league players. J Sports Sci. 2021. DOI: 10.1080/02640414.2021.1875613.
abstract: This prospective cohort study of 125 male professional AFL players from three clubs examined associations between pre-season Nordic hamstring strength, player age, and lower limb injury history with subsequent hamstring injury during one AFL season. Nordic strength was not significantly associated with future hamstring injury, while age greater than 25 years, prior hamstring injury, prior groin injury, and prior calf injury were all significantly associated with increased hamstring injury risk.
tldr: In professional AFL players, pre-season Nordic hamstring strength does not predict hamstring injury risk, but older age and history of prior hamstring, groin, or calf injury are strong independent risk factors that should guide targeted injury prevention screening.
methods: Prospective cohort study; 125 male AFL players from 3 professional clubs. Pre-season bilateral Nordic hamstring strength assessed via Nordbord device. Injury history collected via standardized questionnaire. Hamstring injuries tracked prospectively during one AFL season. Chi-square analysis and odds ratios used.
findings:
• Nordic strength was not significantly associated with future hamstring injury (OR 1.9, p=0.36)
• Age ≥25 years significantly associated with hamstring injury (OR 2.9, p<0.05)
• Prior hamstring injury within 1 year (OR 3.7, p=0.01) and >1 year ago (OR 3.6, p=0.01) both significantly elevated risk
• Prior groin injury (OR 8.6, p<0.01) was the strongest single risk factor
• Prior calf injury (OR 4.6, p=0.01) also significantly increased risk
• Biceps femoris long head was injured in 73% of cases; running was the mechanism in 64%
limitations: Recruited from only three AFL clubs; small number of injury events limits statistical power; self-reported injury history is susceptible to recall bias; multivariate analysis not feasible due to small injury numbers.
practicalImplications: Athletes with a history of hamstring, groin, or calf injury and those aged 25 or older should be flagged as high-risk for hamstring injury regardless of Nordic strength levels. Injury history data is more useful than pre-season strength testing alone for identifying at-risk athletes.
athleteDev: For American football (using Australian football as an analog for high-speed multi-directional running demands), player age and lower limb injury history are critical screening variables. Strength-based screening alone is insufficient; comprehensive injury history profiling is essential in pre-season athlete development planning.
rtp: Prior hamstring, groin, and calf injury dramatically elevate re-injury risk (3.6–8.6x), underscoring the need for robust rehabilitation completion and conservative RTP criteria beyond symptom resolution or strength normalization alone. Rehabilitation of anatomically unrelated lower limb injuries (groin, calf) should include hamstring injury prevention as a secondary goal.

citation: Rowson B, Duma SM. A review of on-field investigations into the biomechanics of concussion in football and translation to head injury mitigation strategies. Ann Biomed Eng. 2020;48(12):2734-2750.
